title: Events & EventEmitter
summary: Node's event-driven core — emit named events and register listeners with the EventEmitter pattern.

blocks.md: Node is **event-driven**: much of its API is built on the idea of *emitting* named events and *listening* for them. Servers, streams, and many core objects are `EventEmitter`s under the hood. Understanding the pattern unlocks how Node's building blocks talk to each other.

blocks.caption: emit and listen

blocks.md: `on(event, listener)` subscribes; `emit(event, ...args)` fires it, calling every registered listener synchronously with the arguments you pass. `once(event, listener)` runs a listener a single time, then removes it.

blocks.caption: extend EventEmitter

blocks.title: The special 'error' event
blocks.md: If an EventEmitter emits an `'error'` event and **no listener** is registered for it, Node throws and can crash the process. Always attach an `.on('error', ...)` listener to emitters that might emit errors (like streams and servers).

blocks.question: What happens when you call `bus.emit('order', 'latte')` with two 'order' listeners registered?
options: It throws because there are too many listeners
blocks.explanation: emit() calls every listener registered for that event, in the order they were added, passing along the arguments. So both 'order' listeners run with 'latte'.

keyTakeaways: Node is event-driven; many core objects (servers, streams) are EventEmitters.
`on(event, fn)` subscribes; `emit(event, ...args)` fires all listeners synchronously.
`once(event, fn)` runs a listener a single time.
Extend EventEmitter to give your own classes emit/on.
Always handle the 'error' event — an unhandled one can crash the process.

exercise.prompt: Create an EventEmitter `chat`, register a listener for a `'message'` event that logs `'💬 ' + text`, then emit a `'message'` with the text `'hello'`.
